Tafseer:
The upheaval of the Day of Resurrection:
The new world that will come into existence after the Resurrection will be based on new laws; therefore, the heaven and the earth that exist today will end, and in their place, as clarified in the Qur'an, another heaven and earth will appear.
Through the words 'When the sky has split open' (idha-s-sama'u-nshaqqat), the same subject is described here as was expressed in Surah Al-Infitar by: (When the sky splits open) (82:1). It is impossible to estimate the scale of this greatest of cosmic events today. The Qur'an mentions this upheaval to shake those foolish individuals who boasted of their fortresses and palaces, unable to comprehend how any rift could appear in them. They are warned: let alone your shelters, this entire firm roof of the sky—in which you cannot find a single flaw today—will become completely filled with rifts and cracks.
Do not distress your mind here with the question of whether this sky we see is merely a void or a solid entity; rather, believe that just as you witness it today as a firm ceiling in which no crack can be pointed out, similarly at the time of the Resurrection's upheaval, it will appear filled with cracks.

'And has responded to its Lord and was obligated [to do so]' (wa adhinat li-rabbiha wa huqqat) - 'Adhina lahu' means: 'istama'a lahu', i.e., he listened to him, obeyed his command, and submitted to him.
'Huqqa lahu' means: it is obligatory upon him to do so, and it is only fitting for him to perform this action.
The meaning is: do not remain in the ignorance of assuming that no one can possess the power to devastate massive entities like the heavens and earth. On that Day, they will split open by the command of their Lord. Allah Almighty will command them, and they will obey His command without hesitation.
'And was obligated' - That is: it is obligatory upon it to do so. Since Allah Almighty created it, how can it be fitting for it to disobey its Creator!
A timely warning: This phrase is revealed here to warn and instruct those arrogant individuals who were determined to oppose Allah and His Messenger at every step. They are timely reminded: the heaven will split open in obedience to its Lord's command, and this is what is fitting for it. Now, those foolish individuals whose status under this heaven is like an insignificant speck should consider how their attitude can be justified when they rise to oppose their Lord, assuming that no one can shake them from their position!

The state of the earth on the Day of Resurrection:
After the heaven, this describes what will occur on the earth—showing that the earth will be stretched. That is: while today the earth contains many rifts, slopes, valleys, mountains, and rivers, and its depths contain countless hidden things, on that Day, it will be stretched like a sheet, and whatever lies within its folds will be cast out, leaving it empty. Although this refers primarily to the resurrection of the dead, the style of expression is general, encompassing everything buried in the earth—including the treasures of the capitalists who are specifically addressed in this Surah.

'And has cast out what was within it and become empty' (wa alqat ma feeha wa takhallat) - The style of expression suggests that the earth is burdened with these things just as a pregnant woman is burdened with her child. Thus, in discharging this burden, it will experience the same relief and ease that a pregnant woman experiences after giving birth.

'And has responded to its Lord and was obligated' - This was explained above. This shows that both the heaven and the earth will obey their Lord's command on that Day, and this obedience is what is fitting for them, and it will be completely voluntary. Elsewhere, this voluntary obedience is described in these words:
(He said to it and to the earth, 'Come [into being], willingly or by compulsion.' They said, 'We have come willingly.') (Fussilat, 41:11).

Warning to the deluded of the world:
Although the wording is general, the focus is specifically on those arrogant individuals who were lost in their worldly pleasures and completely untroubled by the Hereafter. It is stated: O man, you are indeed traveling toward your Lord with a constant striving, and ultimately your presentation must be before Him, whether you are aware of it or not.
The worshippers of the world remain heedless of their ultimate destination due to their worldly success. In achieving one success after another, they become so lost that they cannot reflect on anything else. In the constant run to achieve one success after another, they never find the opportunity to reflect on what their true destination is. They regard some success in this world as their ultimate destination, whereas the ultimate destination is the Hereafter, toward which everyone is traveling helplessly, bound by the chains of God's law. If they kept this in view, they would not deviate from the straight path, but would see with open eyes that while they are running toward their worldly desires, their lives are advancing even faster toward God for the account of their deeds.
Every human being is traveling toward God with a constant striving: 'Indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with [great] exertion' (innaka kadihun ila rabbika kadhan) - 'Kadh' means striving to perform a task with complete effort and exertion. This is a highly eloquent description of the reality that from the day a human comes into existence, his journey toward the destination of death begins, and this journey continues without any pause. Whether the season is harsh or pleasant, whether the person is ill or healthy, under all circumstances, it never stops. From birth to death, various stages—childhood, youth, maturity, and old age—occur, but not a single second's pause occurs. Human beings are so bound by the chains of the law of nature that even if they do not wish to travel, they must do so, and in this helplessness, king and beggar, noble and low, rich and subject, righteous and wicked, are all equal.
'And you will meet Him' (fa-mulaqeeh) - This states the end of this journey. It is stated: man is not an unbridled beast in this world; therefore, it is inevitable that everyone reaches before his Creator to be presented. The purpose of this presentation, obviously, is to be questioned whether they fulfilled the purpose for which Allah created them, as detailed next.

This details the preceding summary. It is stated: on that Day, those whose records of deeds are given in their right hands will have an easy account. The meaning is: receiving their records in their right hands is itself the guarantee of their success—showing that their virtues have triumphed over their sins, making them worthy of pardon and forgiveness. This shows that while their account will indeed be taken, their good deeds will weigh heavier; therefore, their minor errors will be overlooked, whereas those whose bad deeds weigh heavier will have their every error seized and will face punishment.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7 and 8 was combined in the original text.]

A comprehensive style and its implications:
This describes their reward in a comprehensive style: on that Day, they will return to their people in joy. This style encompasses several aspects that are obvious:
- It shows that Allah Almighty will gather them and their believing families together in Paradise—even if the family members did not deserve that high rank on their own—so that they can enjoy the success of Paradise together. Surah At-Tur, verse 21, clarified that for this gathering, Allah will not lower the rank of the righteous, but will elevate the rank of their family members.
- It shows that they did not live among their families heedless of their Hereafter, but cared for their eternal success more than their worldly success. The attitude of those heedless of the Hereafter is described in the previous Surah: (And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting) (Al-Mutaffifeen, 83:31), assuming this green garden of life would never face autumn. In contrast, those who believe in the Hereafter are described in Surah At-Tur, verses 26-27, as saying with joy when gathered with their families in Paradise: 'It was Allah's great favor that we were not heedless of their Hereafter, but remained anxious for it, and as a reward, He has gathered them with us.' (They will say, 'Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful of punishment. So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Wind.')
- It shows that the true requirement of loving one's family is to remain anxious for their Hereafter and to keep them anxious as well. This concern will yield the true joy of gathering and love in the Hereafter. If love was based solely on this world, ignoring the Hereafter, it will ultimately become a source of misery, and instead of rejoicing, they will curse one another.

The end of those heedless of the Hereafter:
This describes the end of the second group—those who lived completely heedless of the Hereafter. It is stated: their records of deeds will be given to them behind their backs in their left hands. Although the left hand is not explicitly mentioned, the context indicates it. Since the first group was described as receiving their records in their right hands, this group's receiving their records in their left hands is to show the unfortunate nature of their deeds. Furthermore, Surah Al-Haqqah, verse 25, explicitly states that this group will receive their records in their left hands:
(But as for he who is given his record in his left hand, he will say, 'Oh, I wish I had not been given my record').
Reading this verse in the light of the verse of Surah Al-Haqqah, we learn that this group will face two humiliations simultaneously: first, receiving their records in their left hands; second, receiving them from behind their backs. Reflecting on this, it also shows that their hands will be bound behind their backs like criminals.

While the believers were described as returning to their families in joy, this group is described as calling out for death and ruin. That is: the only escape they will see from the Fire of Hell is that death should end their existence, but death will not come to relieve them.
Although 'He will cry out for destruction' (yad'oo thuboora) should grammatically follow 'And burn in a Blaze' (yasla sa'eera), placing the effect before the cause makes the contrast between the joy of the first group and the misery of the second group more powerful.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 11 and 12 was combined in the original text.]

'Indeed, he had been among his people joyful' (innahu kana fi ahlihi masroora) - That is: they will be deprived of the joy of gathering with their families in the Hereafter because they had already exhausted their joy in the world. Since they cared neither for their own Hereafter nor for their families' Hereafter, how can they be entitled to enjoy gathering in Paradise? To enjoy any blessing in the Hereafter, a sacrifice must have been offered for its sake in the world.

That is: they assumed that after death, they would neither be revived nor return to anyone; so why should they disturb their worldly pleasures? They enjoyed whatever pleasure they could from life and its resources, without making any preparation for the Hereafter, in which they have no share.

A timely qualification:
This is a timely qualification refuting their assumption mentioned above, showing that their assumption was completely false. The actual reality is: their Lord was ever observing them; and since He was observing them, how could it be fitting for Him not to bring them back for presentation? This would have been contrary to His power, wisdom, justice, and mercy! This proof of the Resurrection and recompense has been explained in various places in the Qur'an.

Testimony in the form of oaths in support of the claim:
Finally, swearing by three things, He has proved the claim stated in verse 6: (O man, indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with great exertion, and you will meet Him). The subject of these oaths is 'You will surely embark upon state after state' (latarkabunna tabaqan 'an tabaq)—showing that while your presentation before God is certain, it will occur through a gradual process, because the divine law in this universe is that everything reaches its completion gradually.
Keeping this summary in mind, reflect on the parts of the discourse:
'So I swear by the twilight glow, and by the night and what it envelops' (fa-la uqsimu bi-sh-shafaq, wa-l-layli wa ma wasaq) - The particle 'la' appearing before the oath is to refute the listener's assumption with force. We repeatedly explain that this style is used to refute the listener's assumption immediately, before stating the proof, showing that the speaker is not willing to delay its refutation. This is a natural style present in every language.
Swearing by the night and its envelopments: First, an oath is sworn by the twilight glow (shafaq), then by the night and what it envelops. 'Shafaq' refers to the red glow on the horizon immediately after sunset, which serves as the introduction to the night. When it disappears, the night covers the world.
'And what it envelops' (wa ma wasaq) - Following the night, He swore by what the night gathers and envelops. The lexicographers have explained 'wasaq' as: what it gathers and brings together. Commentators have generally interpreted this as animals and other creatures that seek refuge in the night. However, the connection of these things with the subject of the oath is not clear, whereas an oath is sworn to prove the subject. Therefore, in our view, this refers to the stars and planets that appear at night and decorate its canopy. First, these are specific characteristics of the night; hence, 'and what it envelops' is highly appropriate for them. Second, the Qur'an repeatedly presents their rising, setting, and movements as testimony to the reality that they are subservient to the Lord of the heavens and earth, moving in a specified path with a strict discipline, and then setting—showing that they are bound by the divine law to which man is directed by: (Indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with great exertion).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 16 and 17 was combined in the original text.]

'And by the moon when it becomes full' (wa-l-qamari idha-ttasaq) - This is a specific mention after a general mention. While 'and what it envelops' encompasses all stars and planets, the moon is selected specifically to highlight its development and waning, because the reality of: (Indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with great exertion) (verse 6) and (You will surely embark upon state after state) is displayed most clearly in its phases. Indeed, the Qur'an has pointed to this aspect of the moon in Surah Ya-Sin:
(And the moon—We have determined for it phases, until it returns like the old dry palm branch) (Ya-Sin, 36:39).
'When it becomes full' (idha-ttasaq) - This points to its completion, showing how it must ascend the climbs phase by phase. It cannot deviate from this, and having reached its peak, it cannot stop there, but must wane phase by phase, from which it has no escape.

'You will surely embark upon state after state' (latarkabunna tabaqan 'an tabaq) - This is the subject of the oaths. It is stated: just as these things are bound by His law and are traveling toward Him, you will likewise cover state after state on your journey toward God. It is impossible to remain fixed or deviate into another direction; but since this will occur gradually in accordance with the divine law, you should not be hasty or deny the reality because it is not shown to you immediately.
This expresses amazement at their condition: despite such obvious signs, what is the matter with them that they do not believe in the Hereafter and recompense!

Expressing amazement at human ungratefulness:
That is: when the Qur'an is informing them of such a great reality, they should have fallen in prostration before their Lord in gratitude upon hearing it; but instead, they behave arrogantly and deny it.
Note the pre-Islamic Arab and Egyptian practice: when they wanted to express deep appreciation and acknowledgment of the grandeur of someone's words, they would immediately fall in prostration. The magicians Pharaoh gathered did the same upon seeing Moses' miracles. This shows that the Qur'an is far superior in its eloquence and truth, but those who did not appreciate its value mocked it instead of prostrating.

This describes the actual situation—showing that instead of prostrating, those who deny the Resurrection are rejecting the Qur'an, claiming it is merely the Prophet's own fabrication to intimidate them.

That is: what they are accumulating in their hearts by rejecting this gem of the Qur'an is well known to Allah. The meaning is: their love for the immediate world has made them reject the Qur'an, but they are unaware of the true value of what they are accumulating, which is known only to Allah. On the Day when their accumulation appears in its true form, they will realize what they threw away and what they accumulated! 'Of what they keep within themselves' (bima yoo'oon) encompasses their wealth, deeds, and the inevitable consequences of their deeds, the true reality of which will manifest in the Hereafter.

It is stated: if they, trapped in their worldly love, lack the courage to choose the path shown by the Qur'an, then give them the glad tidings of a painful punishment whose causes they have fully arranged. The meaning is: since they are not ready to listen to the glad tidings of salvation, give them the glad tidings of punishment. Only those will be saved from this punishment who choose the path of faith and righteous deeds, for whom there is indeed an uninterrupted reward.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 24 and 25 was combined in the original text.]
